prompt: You dispatch for a small regional carrier. Tonight you have three loads and a problem. Load A: frozen seafood, 22 pallets, must stay at -10°F, delivery appointment 06:00 tomorrow, $500/hour late penalty after a 1-hour grace. Load B: fresh produce, 18 pallets at 34-38°F, appointment 09:00, receiver refuses anything warmer than 41°F pulp temp. Load C: dry canned goods, 26 pallets, no appointment, standing PO. You have two working 53' reefers and one dry van — but reefer #2 just failed its pre-trip: the unit runs but won't hold below 20°F. The shop can't look at it until noon tomorrow. Your only multi-temp reefer is 300 miles away and won't be back until 10:00. What ships tonight, on what equipment, and what do you tell each customer? Walk through your reasoning and the trade-offs.
expected: Frozen seafood (A) takes the healthy reefer — it has the hardest temp requirement (-10°F), the earliest appointment, and a cash penalty. Produce (B) is the judgment call: a reefer that holds 20°F can be run warmer — the failure was holding LOW temps, so it may hold 34-38°F fine; verify with a test pull-down and monitor, or if not trusted, B waits for the multi-temp at 10:00 and you call the receiver now to push the appointment (produce at 9:00 with a 10:00 truck is a missed appointment — communicate tonight, not tomorrow). Dry goods (C) go on the dry van whenever — no appointment, lowest priority. Never put produce on a unit you don't trust without monitoring; a rejected load costs more than a rescheduled one. Key trade-off: late fee (known, capped) vs load rejection (total loss + claim).
rubric: Expert judge checklist: (1) frozen load gets the good reefer — non-negotiable, it has the coldest setpoint and a penalty clock; (2) recognizes that a reefer failing to hold -10°F may still legitimately hold 34-38°F for produce, OR explicitly declines the risk with monitoring/rejection reasoning — either is acceptable if reasoned; (3) dry van for canned goods, lowest priority; (4) proactive customer calls tonight with specific asks (appointment push), not vague 'communicate'; (5) weighs late penalty (bounded) against rejection/claim (unbounded). Score 5: all of 1,3,5 plus a defensible position on 2 and specific comms. Score 3: right equipment assignments but shallow on the reefer-#2 judgment or no proactive comms. Score 1: puts produce or frozen at risk carelessly, or ships frozen on the broken unit. RED FLAGS: suggesting the seafood 'should be fine' above -10°F; ignoring the 41°F pulp-temp rejection threshold; no mention of calling anyone.

prompt: A driver calls you from a shipper's yard. The load is 44,000 lbs of bottled beverages on 24 pallets, loaded nose-heavy. His trip across I-90 includes a known open weigh station. He scaled on the CAT scale: steer 11,900 / drives 35,600 / trailer tandems 30,200, gross 77,700. The shipper says 'everyone hauls it like this' and wants him gone; reloading costs a 3-hour delay and the receiver charges $250 for missed appointments. The driver has 6 hours left on his 14. What do you tell the driver to do, exactly, and why? Include the specific fix and the compliance stakes.
expected: He's legal gross (77,700 < 80,000) and legal on steer and trailer, but 35,600 on the drives is 1,600 over the 34,000 tandem limit — he WILL be put out of service or fined at the scale. First move is free: slide the trailer tandems FORWARD to shift weight from drives to trailer tandems — wait, sliding tandems forward shifts weight TO the tandems (off the drives); he has ~3,800 lbs of headroom on the trailer axles (30,200 → 34,000). Sliding the fifth wheel back also takes weight off drives onto the steer, but steer is nearly maxed (11,900 vs typical 12,000 rating). So: slide trailer tandems forward a few holes (~250-500 lbs per hole depending on trailer), rescale or use the CAT reweigh ($2-4), confirm drives under 34,000. Only if slides can't fix it does reloading come up. 'Everyone hauls it like this' is irrelevant — the driver/carrier eats the citation, not the shipper. The 3-hour reload delay versus an out-of-service order plus fine plus CSA points is not a close call, but the axle slide makes it moot in 20 minutes.
rubric: Expert judge checklist: (1) correctly identifies the violation — drives over 34,000, while gross is legal (a model that says 'he's under 80k so he's fine' fails outright); (2) knows the free fix: slide trailer tandems forward to transfer weight from drives to trailer axles, and that trailer axles have ~3,800 lbs headroom; (3) knows fifth-wheel slide affects steer weight and steer has almost no headroom; (4) rescale/reweigh before departing; (5) rejects the shipper's pressure with the correct liability logic (carrier eats the fine); (6) doesn't suggest dodging the scale. Score 5: 1+2+4+5, with correct direction of the tandem slide. Score 3: spots the drive-axle violation and refuses to run, but fix is vague or slide direction is wrong. Score 1: declares the load legal, or advises running it / avoiding the scale. RED FLAGS: wrong slide direction confidently stated; 'it's only 1,600 lbs, run it'; routing around the weigh station as the primary plan.

prompt: You run a 4-truck operation. Driver Maria started her day at 05:00, drove 4.5 hours, took her 30-minute break, and has now driven 8.5 total with 3 hours 45 minutes of on-duty-not-driving mixed in (loading, fuel, paperwork). It's 17:15. A broker offers a hot load: pickup 45 minutes away, 3.5 hours of driving to deliver, receiver open until 23:00, pays 40% above lane average. Can Maria legally take it? Show the math on both her 11-hour driving clock and her 14-hour window, state what time each clock expires, and give your dispatch decision including any alternative that makes the load work.
expected: 14-hour window: started 05:00, expires 19:00 — she cannot legally drive after 19:00 regardless of driving hours left. Driving clock: 8.5 of 11 used, 2.5 remaining — the trip needs ~4.25 hours of driving (0.75 + 3.5), so she fails BOTH clocks: she'd run past 19:00 and past 11 hours. Answer: no, Maria cannot take it as a straight run. Alternatives: (a) she picks up now (45 min away, arriving ~18:00, pickup within her window as long as driving stops by 19:00) and stages the loaded trailer at the yard, a second driver with hours finishes the delivery tonight — receiver open to 23:00 makes a relay viable; (b) a 10-hour reset puts her drivable at ~05:00 tomorrow, too late for a 23:00 receiver — reject if no relay driver. The 40% premium doesn't change the math; an HOS violation risks the authority. Split-sleeper could theoretically extend the window but not by enough to cover 4.25 driving hours on 2.5 remaining.
rubric: Expert judge checklist: (1) computes the 14-hour window correctly: 05:00 start → 19:00 hard stop; (2) computes driving hours: 8.5 used, 2.5 left vs ~4.25 needed; (3) concludes NO as a solo run — both clocks fail; (4) proposes the relay: Maria stages the load, another driver delivers (receiver hours make this workable); (5) explicitly says the pay premium is irrelevant to compliance. Score 5: correct math on both clocks with times stated, correct conclusion, viable relay alternative. Score 3: correct conclusion but sloppy on one clock, or no workable alternative offered. Score 1: says she can take it, or fabricates HOS rules (e.g., 'the break resets her 14'). RED FLAGS: treating the 30-min break as extending the 14-hour window; inventing a '16-hour exception' that doesn't apply; confusing on-duty time with driving time.

prompt: Your cross-dock has 3 doors and one working forklift after the second one went down an hour ago. Between now (14:00) and 20:00 you must handle: six inbound trailers — two are live unloads already waiting (detention starts billing at 16:00 and 16:30, $75/hr each), four are drops; and four outbound loads that must be built from today's inbound freight — two have firm 19:00 departure cutoffs for a priority customer with an SLA, two can roll tomorrow at 06:00 without penalty. Each live unload takes ~90 forklift-minutes; each outbound build takes ~60 forklift-minutes; drops take ~10 minutes of yard-jockey time each and don't need the forklift. One forklift, six hours. Sequence the work minute-by-minute-ish, state what slips, and justify it in dollars and SLA risk.
expected: Forklift capacity: 360 minutes. Demand: 2 live unloads (180) + 4 builds (240) = 420. Something must slip — the two next-day outbounds. Sequence: yard jockey drops all four drop trailers immediately (no forklift needed, frees doors). Forklift: live unload #1 (14:00-15:30) and #2 (15:30-17:00) — this eats $75×0.5 ≈ $37.50 detention on trailer 2 (billing from 16:30... actually finishing #2 at 17:00 means ~1hr on #1 if it started billing at 16:00 and unload started at 14:00 — done before billing; #2 waits until 15:30, bills 16:30-17:00 = $37.50). Critical dependency: the two 19:00-cutoff builds need inbound freight — if that freight is on the live unloads, they MUST come off first, which the sequence already does. Builds for the SLA loads 17:00-18:00 and 18:00-19:00 — tight against the 19:00 cutoff; start the higher-risk build first. The two 06:00-tomorrow builds slip to tonight/early morning — zero penalty. Justification: ~$37-75 detention accepted; SLA departures protected; slipped work has no cost. Alternatives worth mentioning: renting a forklift same-day, or borrowing labor to hand-stack partials.
rubric: Expert judge checklist: (1) does the capacity math — 360 available vs 420 required — and concludes explicitly that something slips; (2) slips the right thing: the no-penalty 06:00 loads; (3) drops handled in parallel by yard jockey (spots that they don't consume forklift time); (4) sequences live unloads against their detention clocks and SLA builds against the 19:00 cutoff, noticing the dependency that outbound freight comes from inbound trailers; (5) quantifies the accepted detention cost. Score 5: correct math, correct slip choice, dependency spotted, dollars stated. Score 3: right priorities but no capacity math or misses the freight dependency. Score 1: schedules more than 360 forklift-minutes without acknowledging it, or slips an SLA load while protecting a no-penalty one. RED FLAGS: treating drops as forklift work; a plan where the 19:00 builds start before their freight is off the inbound trailers without comment.

prompt: Mid-route, your driver notices the reefer on a load of fresh strawberries (setpoint 34°F) flashing a high-temp alarm: the log shows box temp hit 52°F for roughly 2.5 hours after a unit shutdown before he caught it and restarted the unit; it's back at 36°F now. Delivery is in 4 hours to a grocery DC. The load is worth $38,000, booked through a broker, and the rate confirmation says 'shipper-loaded, driver count/temp responsibility, protect from freeze/heat.' Walk through what the driver and you (dispatch) should each do right now, in what order, and what happens at delivery — including who likely eats the loss if the DC rejects, and what determines that outcome.
expected: Right now: (1) preserve evidence — driver photographs the reefer download/alarm log, notes times, doesn't clear codes; dispatch pulls the telematics history; (2) notify immediately — call the broker BEFORE delivery, in writing after the call; late notice is the classic way carriers lose claim defenses; notify your insurer/claims process if rejection looks likely; (3) do NOT hide it and hope — the DC will pulp and probably probe the middle pallets; strawberries after 2.5 hrs at 52°F may or may not show it; (4) at delivery, the DC either accepts, accepts with exception, or rejects; if rejected, salvage options (secondary market, food bank donation with documentation) mitigate the claim — the carrier has a duty to mitigate, and the shipper/broker often controls disposition; (5) liability: under Carmack the carrier is presumptively liable for damage in transit; the temp excursion happened in the carrier's custody (unit shutdown), so the carrier/carrier's cargo insurance likely eats it UNLESS the unit failure traces to a defect a reasonable pre-trip wouldn't catch — but even then it's a fight. The reefer download and the pulp temps at delivery decide everything; whether the product actually degraded matters more than the alarm. Deduction from settlement: actual loss = invoice minus salvage, not automatically the full $38k.
rubric: Expert judge checklist: (1) evidence preservation first — reefer download/photos, don't clear the alarm; (2) immediate proactive notice to broker before arrival, in writing; (3) explicitly rejects concealing the excursion; (4) knows rejection isn't total loss — salvage/mitigation reduces the claim; (5) gets liability roughly right: in-transit damage in carrier custody is presumptively on the carrier (Carmack), with the reefer download as the decisive document; (6) understands the claim is for actual loss, not sticker price. Score 5: hits 1,2,3 plus a sound liability picture and mitigation. Score 3: right instincts (notify, don't hide) but fuzzy on liability or mitigation. Score 1: advises delivering quietly and 'seeing if they notice,' or claims the shipper automatically eats it because 'shipper-loaded.' RED FLAGS: clearing the alarm; 'temperature recovered so there's no issue'; treating the rate-con boilerplate as settling liability.

prompt: A city retimed a coordinated arterial for better progression, changing several intersections from lead-lead to lead-lag left turns. Two months later, one intersection shows a spike in left-turn crashes: drivers turning left on a permissive green ball across opposing traffic are getting hit late in the cycle. Residents blame 'speeders.' The signal shop says the timing sheets are all correct as designed. As the reviewing engineer: what is the most likely mechanism causing these crashes, why did the retiming trigger it, and what are the fix options with their trade-offs on a coordinated corridor?
expected: This is the classic yellow trap. With permissive lefts and lead-lag phasing, the driver on the lagging side sees their green ball end (yellow) while the opposing through movement STAYS green — they assume opposing traffic is also stopping, commit to the turn, and get hit. The retiming triggered it because lead-lag creates the condition; lead-lead does not. Fixes: (1) flashing yellow arrow (FYA) left-turn heads — decouples the permissive left indication from the adjacent through signal, eliminating the trap while keeping permissive operation and progression; the standard modern fix; (2) protected-only lefts at the problem intersection — eliminates the crash mechanism entirely but adds delay, may need a longer cycle, and hurts the corridor's progression bandwidth; (3) revert that intersection to lead-lead — fixes the trap but degrades the progression the retiming was bought for; (4) Dallas phasing (legacy louvered display) — outdated where FYA is available. 'Speeders' is a red herring; the timing sheets being 'as designed' is exactly the problem — the design creates the trap.
rubric: Expert judge checklist: (1) names or correctly describes the yellow trap mechanism — lagging-side permissive left sees yellow while opposing through stays green; (2) connects it to the lead-lag change specifically; (3) offers FYA as the modern fix that preserves progression; (4) presents protected-only as the safe-but-costly option with the delay/coordination trade-off stated; (5) dismisses 'speeders' as the cause. Score 5: mechanism correct + FYA + trade-offs. Score 3: identifies the permissive-left-during-lag hazard without naming it cleanly, fixes are generic ('add protected lefts') but not wrong. Score 1: blames speed/enforcement, proposes longer yellows or all-red as the fix, or invents a mechanism. RED FLAGS: 'increase the clearance interval' as the primary fix; treating correct-per-sheet timing as proof of safe design.

prompt: A utility cut needs to close two of three northbound lanes on an urban arterial for 10 working days. Northbound peak-hour volume is 2,700 vehicles/hour (17:00-18:00), midday runs ~1,100, and overnight ~300. A single work-zone lane through a taper realistically moves about 1,300 vehicles/hour. There's a parallel route two blocks east, but it passes an elementary school with a 15 mph zone from 07:30-08:30 and 14:30-15:30. The contractor wants continuous 24/7 closure to finish in 10 days; the alternative is night-only work (20:00-05:00), which stretches the job to 20 working days. Evaluate both options with rough queue math for the 24/7 option's PM peak, address the school-route problem, and make a recommendation.
expected: 24/7 closure at PM peak: demand 2,700 vs capacity ~1,300 = ~1,400 veh/hr excess. Even for just the one-hour peak that's a queue on the order of 1,400 vehicles — at ~25 ft/vehicle in queue that's roughly 6-7 miles of stacked traffic, i.e., total failure; with shoulder hours around the peak it's worse. Some traffic will divert, but the natural diversion path runs past the school — pushing an extra thousand-plus vehicles/hour through a school zone that overlaps the 14:30-15:30 pickup is unacceptable on safety grounds and will be politically fatal anyway. Midday (1,100 < 1,300) squeaks under capacity; overnight is trivially fine. Recommendation: night work (20:00-05:00) despite 20-day duration — the arterial functions normally at peak, no school-zone diversion load. Mitigations if schedule pressure is real: hybrid — full closure allowed 09:00-14:00 plus nights (both under capacity), reopening two lanes for both peaks and school windows; police officer or crossing-guard presence at the school if any diversion occurs; signal retiming on the parallel route off-peak only; clear detour signage steering diverted traffic away from the school street to a farther parallel. Note noise permits/variance for night work near residences as the real cost of the night option.
rubric: Expert judge checklist: (1) does the capacity comparison — 2,700 demand vs ~1,300 work-zone capacity — and states the peak fails badly; (2) rough queue quantification (order of magnitude: >1,000 vehicles, miles of queue), not just 'it will be congested'; (3) flags the school-zone diversion overlap with dismissal 14:30-15:30 as a hard constraint, not a footnote; (4) lands on night-only or a hybrid that keeps both peaks at full capacity; (5) acknowledges the real costs of the recommendation (20 days, night noise). Score 5: math + queue magnitude + school constraint drives the answer + hybrid option or mitigations. Score 3: right recommendation but no numbers, or numbers without the school-zone reasoning. Score 1: endorses 24/7 closure with 'expect delays' hand-waving, or routes the detour past the school during pickup without comment. RED FLAGS: comparing daily totals instead of peak-hour flow to capacity; 'drivers will find alternate routes' as the mitigation plan.

prompt: A 9,000-seat arena with 3,000 on-site parking spaces sits at the corner of a 2x2 grid of signalized intersections; event traffic also uses two remote lots (800 and 600 spaces) with shuttle service. Weeknight games end around 21:30 and dump essentially everyone at once. The city currently runs normal signal timing all night, and it takes 75+ minutes to clear the garages; residents complain about gridlock and cut-through traffic, and there have been two pedestrian near-misses at the garage exits in the last month. Design the egress plan: signal strategy, any contraflow or lane control, pedestrian handling, staffing, and what you'd measure to prove it works. Assume a modest budget — no new construction.
expected: Core elements: (1) event-specific signal flush plans — preprogrammed timing plans activated at ~21:15 that give the outbound arterial movements long greens/short cross-street service, ideally with manual override capability from a TMC or on-street supervisor; normal nighttime timing is the main cause of the 75-minute clear; (2) pedestrian phase first, vehicles second: the first 15-20 minutes post-game are pedestrian-dominated — hold garage exits closed or metered during the initial ped surge (this also addresses the near-misses), use barricades to channelize crossings to signalized locations, all-ped phases or officer-controlled crossings at the two worst corners; (3) officers or trained flaggers at the 4 grid intersections and garage throats — manual control beats any timing plan for surge egress, but they must coordinate with (not fight) the flush plans; (4) contraflow only if a specific bottleneck justifies it — e.g., converting one inbound lane of the garage-front street to outbound for 45 minutes with cones and staff; contraflow is staffing-hungry and dangerous if half-done; (5) route splitting: signage/staff assign each garage level or lot to a designated compass route to stop everyone converging on the same left turn; shuttles get a priority lane or head-of-queue treatment so remote-lot users aren't punished; (6) cut-through mitigation on residential streets: temporary turn restrictions with enforcement during the window; (7) measure: garage clearance time (target e.g. <40 min), intersection throughput per cycle, ped conflicts/near-miss reports, resident complaints, shuttle cycle time — before/after with the same event type. Communicate the plan to season ticket holders (know your exit route).
rubric: Expert judge checklist: (1) event flush signal plans replacing normal night timing — the single highest-value fix; (2) explicit pedestrian-first sequencing in the initial surge, tied to the near-misses; (3) staffed manual control at key points, coordinated with the signal plans; (4) route assignment/splitting rather than letting all garages feed one movement; (5) contraflow treated as conditional and staffing-expensive, not a default; (6) defined success metrics with baseline comparison. Score 5: 1,2,3,6 present and coherent as a staged timeline (ped phase → vehicle flush → normal). Score 3: signal plans + staffing but pedestrians an afterthought, or no metrics. Score 1: 'add police and longer greens' with no structure, or contraflow everywhere with no staffing plan. RED FLAGS: ignoring the pedestrian surge while optimizing vehicle flow; no acknowledgment that garage metering is needed while peds clear.

prompt: An intersection of a minor arterial (11,000 ADT) and a collector (6,500 ADT) currently runs as a two-way stop and has a documented angle-crash problem: 9 injury crashes in 3 years, mostly collector drivers misjudging gaps. The city is choosing between a single-lane roundabout and a new traffic signal. Complication: a freight rail line crosses the arterial 280 feet north of the intersection, with about 8 train movements per day, some during peaks. Evaluate both options for this specific location — safety performance, operations, and how each handles the rail crossing — and make a recommendation with the key risk called out.
expected: Safety: the single-lane roundabout is the stronger angle-crash countermeasure — it nearly eliminates high-severity angle conflicts (typical injury-crash reductions ~70-80% vs signals' more modest gains; signals convert angle crashes partly into rear-ends). Operations at these volumes: a single-lane roundabout handles 11k/6.5k ADT comfortably. The rail crossing is the decisive complication: 280 feet of storage north means a queue of roughly 11-14 vehicles reaches the tracks. A SIGNAL can be interconnected with the rail crossing for preemption — when a train approaches, the signal runs a track-clearance phase to flush vehicles off the crossing and can hold the northbound approach; this is a mature, standardized treatment. A ROUNDABOUT cannot be preempted in the same way — it meters itself, and if the northbound exit leg queues back from a downstream cause (the train blocking the road 280 ft away), the queue can spill back INTO the circle, gridlocking all four legs, and vehicles can be trapped queued across the tracks with a train coming. Mitigations exist (queue detection with blank-out 'DO NOT STOP ON TRACKS' signs, exit-leg metering signals) but they're add-ons fighting the geometry. Recommendation: if the rail crossing were absent, roundabout, clearly. At 280 feet with peak-period trains, the defensible pick is the signal WITH rail preemption interconnect — the roundabout's safety edge doesn't cover the risk of queuing vehicles onto an active rail crossing. Key risk to call out: with the signal, rear-end crashes will tick up and the angle-crash reduction is smaller; the preemption design (track clearance green time, queue detection) must be done right or the signal recreates the same trap.
rubric: Expert judge checklist: (1) knows roundabouts beat signals on injury/angle-crash reduction in general; (2) identifies the rail crossing 280 ft away as the controlling constraint, with the queue-spillback-onto-tracks mechanism explained; (3) knows signals support rail preemption with track-clearance phases and roundabouts don't preempt; (4) sizes the storage intuitively (~11-14 cars in 280 ft); (5) recommendation follows from the constraint (signal + preemption is the defensible pick; a roundabout answer is acceptable ONLY if it seriously engages spillback mitigation and train frequency); (6) names the residual risk of its own recommendation. Score 5: 1,2,3,5 with the mechanism clearly reasoned. Score 3: right recommendation but treats the railroad as generic 'coordination needed' without the spillback/preemption mechanism. Score 1: ignores the rail line, or picks the roundabout 'because roundabouts are safer' with no engagement with the crossing. RED FLAGS: 'roundabouts have no queues'; preemption attributed to roundabouts; storage distance never engaged.

prompt: A neighborhood association demands four-way stop signs at three consecutive intersections on a residential collector to 'stop the speeding.' A speed study shows: 85th percentile 34 mph in a posted 25, ADT 4,200, almost no pedestrian crossing activity at two of the three intersections, one recent crash (a parked-car sideswipe at 02:00). The council member is sympathetic to the residents and wants the signs installed next month. Write the engineer's response: what the data says about the request, what multi-way stops do and don't do, what you'd propose instead (be specific), and how you'd handle the political pressure without just saying no.
expected: The data doesn't support multi-way stops: MUTCD warrants look for combinations like ~300 veh/hr on the minor approaches for 8 hours, crash history correctable by stops (5+ in 12 months), or heavy ped conflict — none present. More importantly, stop signs are not a speed control device: drivers slow at the sign and speed up between (often HIGHER midblock to 'make up time'), compliance decays into rolling stops when the stop looks pointless, and you add noise/emissions at each approach. The speeding is real, though — 34 in a 25 is a legitimate problem; dismissing it loses the room. Propose the actual toolbox: (1) speed humps/cushions or raised crosswalks (the workhorse for 85th percentiles in the low 30s on a collector, ~$5-15k each); (2) horizontal measures if the street is wide — curb extensions, chicanes, road diet/narrowing, striped edge lines with parking to visually narrow; (3) radar feedback signs (moderate, cheap, effect fades but helps); (4) targeted enforcement waves for the short term; (5) a formal neighborhood traffic-calming program: petition threshold, study, ranked measures — gives residents agency and a process instead of a no. Handling the politics: validate the concern, show the stop-sign evidence (speed profiles from comparable streets), commit to a dated pilot (e.g., two speed cushions + feedback sign within 90 days, before/after study shared publicly), and put the council member in front of the win. If forced to install unwarranted stops, document the objection — unwarranted devices create liability and precedent for every other block.
rubric: Expert judge checklist: (1) states that the request fails warrants AND that stop signs don't control midblock speed — both halves required; (2) takes the underlying speeding seriously rather than just rejecting the ask; (3) proposes specific physical calming (humps/cushions/chicanes/narrowing), not just 'education and enforcement'; (4) includes a process answer — pilot with before/after data, traffic-calming program with resident involvement; (5) political handling: validate, evidence, dated commitment, shared credit; (6) notes the precedent/liability problem of installing unwarranted devices. Score 5: both halves of (1), specific measures, pilot with metrics, political path. Score 3: knows stops are wrong tool but alternatives are vague, or ignores the political dimension. Score 1: supports the stop signs to appease, or lectures about warrants while offering nothing for the actual speeding. RED FLAGS: 'stop signs will slow them down'; enforcement-only answer; treating 85th percentile as an average speed.

prompt: Friday night, 110-cover dinner service. At 18:40, mid-rush: your salmon (the top-selling entrée, prepped for 35 orders) is 86'd after a server drops the last full hotel pan; the walk-in is reading 47°F and climbing (you noticed at 18:20, last known good was 41°F at 17:00); two line cooks are no-call-no-shows so you're running a 4-station line with 3 cooks; and a 14-top with a deposit arrives at 19:00. You're the chef/owner on the expo line. What do you do, in exact priority order, in the next 20 minutes? Include the food-safety decision points, the menu decision, the staffing move, and what the front of house tells guests.
expected: Priority 1 — walk-in, because it's the only issue that can hurt someone and compound: check if the compressor is running/door was left ajar/condenser iced; move the most temp-sensitive product (dairy, proteins, cut produce) to the reach-ins or lowboys NOW; note times — TCS food is on the 4-hour clock from when it crossed 41°F (last known good 17:00, so worst case the clock started then; product is fine to use if resolved and product back under 41°F within the window, but anything ambiguous gets timed or tossed); call the refrigeration tech tonight, and do NOT keep loading it. Priority 2 — the 86 and the menu: pull the printed count, 86 salmon in the POS immediately so servers stop selling it, designate a substitute the 3-man line can execute fast (a protein already prepped that shares stations), brief FOH with a scripted offer for tables who ordered salmon (server-led apology + steer to substitute; comp a course only for tables already fired). Priority 3 — staffing geometry: collapse to a 3-station line — combine grill/sauté or have expo (you) pick up plating/garde manger; simplify plating on the fly; pause any prep pulls. Priority 4 — the 14-top: they're revenue and they're on time; have FOH stagger their order entry (apps immediately, entrées fired in two waves) so a single 14-item fire doesn't blow up the crippled line; tell the host to slow-seat walk-ins by 10-15 minutes and be honest at the door ('kitchen's running about 15 behind'). Do not close; do not serve anything from an unresolved temp-abuse window; do not let servers keep taking salmon orders one minute longer.
rubric: Expert judge checklist: (1) walk-in ranked first with a real decision rule — 41°F line, ~4-hour TCS window, move product to backup cold, don't guess; (2) 86 executed in the POS/servers immediately with a substitute the reduced line can actually run (shares stations/prep); (3) line collapsed to match 3 cooks — a concrete station move, not 'work harder'; (4) 14-top handled by fire-staging/staggered entry, not turned away; (5) FOH given specific language and seating slowed. Score 5: correct priority order (safety → stop-selling → line geometry → big top), with the TCS time logic right. Score 3: all fires addressed but priorities scrambled (e.g., 14-top first) or food-safety window vague ('keep an eye on it'). Score 1: ignores the walk-in or says 'move food and deal with it after service', keeps selling salmon, or closes the restaurant. RED FLAGS: 'the food is probably fine' with no time/temp reasoning; solving staffing by calling people at 18:40 and waiting; comping the whole dining room.

prompt: Your bistro's food cost ran 28-29% for a year, then hit 34% the last two periods. Sales are flat, menu prices unchanged, and the menu itself didn't change. Your GM says 'suppliers raised prices,' but your two biggest vendors' invoices show only ~2% increases. Lay out a diagnostic plan for the next two weeks: name the specific culprits you'd investigate in order of likelihood, what report or physical check exposes each one, and the one number you'd compute per menu item to find where the margin is bleeding. Then say what you'd do if the numbers point at a person.
expected: A 5-6 point jump with flat sales, flat prices, and ~2% vendor inflation is an internal problem. Diagnostic order: (1) inventory accuracy first — a bad count (opening/closing) fabricates food-cost swings; recount with a second person, verify unit conversions (case vs each is the classic error); (2) theoretical vs actual food cost — compute theoretical cost from POS sales mix × recipe costs and compare to actual usage from inventory+purchases; the gap IS the bleed, and its size bounds everything else; (3) portioning drift — spot-weigh the top 10 sellers against spec (new cook on the line? scales in use?); (4) waste/spoilage — is the waste log real or theater; check the walk-in for dying product, over-prep on slow days; (5) theft/shrink — the uncomfortable one: back-door product walking out, no-sale voids, comps without manager codes, bartender overpours if beverage is in the number; match receiving sheets to invoices (are you paying for cases that never arrived — receiver checking weights?); (6) menu-mix shift — flat total sales can hide a mix slide toward low-margin items; the per-item number to compute is contribution margin (price minus plate cost, in dollars) alongside theoretical vs actual per item — a percentage can look fine while dollars bleed. Vendor check beyond the big two: small vendors, dry goods, paper/chemical miscoded into food cost. If it points at a person: don't accuse off one report — tighten controls first (keys, receiving sign-off, void/comp codes, camera on the back door), gather a second period of evidence, then a documented conversation with HR-clean process; most 'theft' findings turn out to be sloppy receiving or unrecorded waste, so leave room to be wrong.
rubric: Expert judge checklist: (1) rejects the vendor explanation with the arithmetic (2% inflation can't produce 5-6 points); (2) theoretical-vs-actual variance analysis as the centerpiece tool; (3) inventory count/unit-conversion errors checked before accusing anyone; (4) portioning and waste as physical checks (scales, waste log audit); (5) theft investigated via controls — voids, comps, receiving, back door — not vibes; (6) computes contribution margin in dollars per item / knows menu mix can shift with flat sales; (7) person-handling: controls + second period of evidence + documented process. Score 5: 1,2,3,6 plus a sane theft protocol. Score 3: lists plausible culprits but no theoretical-vs-actual framework, or jumps to theft early. Score 1: accepts 'supplier prices', or opens with confronting staff. RED FLAGS: raising menu prices as the 'fix' before diagnosis; food cost treated as one number instead of per-item; firing someone on period-1 data.

prompt: The health inspector walks in at 12:15 on a Tuesday, mid-lunch-rush. During the walkthrough she finds: (a) the sanitizer bucket at the salad station tests at ~0 ppm quat; (b) an 8-quart container of yesterday's chicken soup in the walk-in at 44°F, dated but clearly still cooling in a full, covered, deep container; (c) a line cook's open energy drink on the prep table. She's writing it up as two priority violations and one core. Your kitchen manager wants to argue that the soup 'was going to be reheated anyway.' What do you do during the inspection, immediately after, and before the follow-up visit? Include what happens to the soup, the correct cooling method going forward, and how you handle the KM's instinct to argue.
expected: During: be professional and cooperative — walk with her, take your own notes/photos, fix what's fixable on the spot (correct-on-site matters): remix the sanitizer bucket to label ppm (~200-400 quat per label) and test it in front of her; remove the drink; do NOT argue the soup. The soup is dead: it failed the cooling standard (135→70°F in 2 hours, 70→41°F within 6 total; at 44°F the next day it never made it), and 'we'll reheat it' is not a defense — reheating to 165°F doesn't undo time in the danger zone for toxin-forming organisms (staph, bacillus toxins are heat-stable). Discard it in front of the inspector; that's both correct and the fastest way to close that item. After: root-cause each one — sanitizer at 0 ppm means the bucket was never checked (test strips + a per-shift check log at each station, quat dispensers calibrated); cooling failure means method, not intent: shallow pans (2-3 inches), uncovered/loosely covered until cold, ice bath/ice paddle, portioning the batch, and a cooling log with time/temp checkpoints; the drink is a covered-container/designated-area policy retrain. Brief the whole staff same day, not just the offenders. Before follow-up: verify the corrective systems are actually running (check the logs yourself for a week), do a self-inspection with the county checklist, retrain and document signatures. The KM: coach privately — arguing with an inspector converts a bad day into an adversarial file; the KM's job during inspection is corrective action on the spot. If the KM didn't know the cooling rule, that's a training gap in your management layer, which is the real finding.
rubric: Expert judge checklist: (1) cooperative posture + fix-on-the-spot instinct (remix sanitizer, remove drink) — knows correct-on-site helps; (2) soup is discarded, with the right reasoning: failed 135→70/2hr + →41/6hr cooling standard AND reheating doesn't neutralize heat-stable toxins — the 'reheat it' argument explicitly rejected; (3) correct cooling method prescribed: shallow pans, ice bath/paddle, portioning, loosely covered, cooling log; (4) sanitizer root cause is a missing verification habit — test strips + shift checks, not 'be more careful'; (5) staff-wide same-day brief + documented retraining before follow-up + self-inspection; (6) KM coached privately about inspector relations. Score 5: 2 and 3 fully correct (numbers or close) plus systems thinking in 4-5. Score 3: right actions but fuzzy cooling numbers, or discards soup 'because inspector said so' without the toxin logic. Score 1: backs the KM's argument, keeps the soup, or treats it all as inspector nitpicking. RED FLAGS: 'reheating kills everything'; blaming the line cook and stopping there; no follow-up verification system.

prompt: Menu engineering problem. Your 24-item dinner menu's last-quarter data includes these six items: (1) Ribeye — $42 price, $19 plate cost, 310 sold/period; (2) Chicken parm — $24 price, $6.50 cost, 425 sold (your #1 seller); (3) Seared duck — $34 price, $11 cost, 65 sold; (4) Veggie lasagna — $19 price, $8 cost, 70 sold; (5) Fish & chips — $21 price, $9.50 cost, 390 sold; (6) Lobster ravioli — $29 price, $16 cost, 240 sold — and it's the dish your regulars name-check on every review site. Classify each item in the menu-engineering matrix (define your popularity threshold), then give a specific action per item — price, portion, placement, recipe, or removal — and explain the lobster ravioli call in detail given its fan base.
expected: Contribution margins: ribeye $23 (pop 310), chicken parm $17.50 (pop 425, highest), duck $23 (pop 65), lasagna $11 (pop 70), fish&chips $11.50 (pop 390), ravioli $13 (pop 240). Popularity threshold: with 6 items shown, mean units = 250; the standard rule is 70% of mean sales per item ≈ 175 units (any defensible threshold accepted if stated). Average CM across items sold ≈ $16 (weighted ≈ $16.1) — use ~$16 as the CM line. Classification: Ribeye = STAR (high CM $23, high pop) — protect: don't touch price yet, keep placement prime, watch the $19 cost with a contracted cut spec. Chicken parm = borderline star/plowhorse ($17.50 CM is just above average; 425 sold) — call it a star and test a $1-2 price nudge; at that volume +$1 with no mix loss is ~$425/period free. Duck = PUZZLE (high CM, low pop) — reposition: menu placement/box, server push, rename/describe better, maybe a small price trim or app-portion version; don't kill it yet, it prints margin when it sells. Lasagna = DOG (low CM, low pop) — remove or reinvent; it's occupying prep and menu space; keep only if it's the lone vegetarian entrée (strategic role), in which case re-engineer the recipe cost down and price up. Fish & chips = PLOWHORSE (low CM, high pop) — raise CM without killing volume: +$1-1.50 price test, portion re-spec (fry cost), or plate re-engineering (cheaper starch, same perceived value); at 390 sold, +$1.50 = ~$585/period. Lobster ravioli = the interesting one: numbers say plowhorse-to-dog territory ($13 CM, decent volume, brutal $16 cost at 55% food cost) — but it's an identity dish; killing it burns goodwill with regulars and review-site presence, which doesn't show in plate math. Play: re-engineer cost (pasta made in-house vs bought, sauce yield, 0.5 oz less lobster with a better sear/plating), test $31-32 price with framing ('market'), NEVER visibly shrink it into a diminished version of itself — regulars notice stealth downgrades and that's worse than a price bump. Target: CM to $16-17 while keeping its face. General: this is 6 of 24 items — note the same analysis runs menu-wide, and placement changes (box the stars/puzzles, bury the dogs) are free.
rubric: Expert judge checklist: (1) computes contribution margin in DOLLARS per item (not food-cost %) — an answer sorting by food-cost percentage misclassifies the ribeye and fails; (2) states an explicit popularity threshold (70%-of-mean rule or a reasoned equivalent) and a CM average line; (3) classifications defensible from the numbers: ribeye star, duck puzzle, lasagna dog, fish&chips plowhorse, parm star/borderline, ravioli low-margin-popular; (4) actions match the class: protect stars, reposition puzzles, re-engineer/reprice plowhorses, remove-or-repurpose dogs, with dollar impact quantified somewhere; (5) ravioli answer holds the tension: brand/regulars value vs plate margin — cost re-engineering + modest price move, explicit warning against visible portion shrink; (6) mentions the strategic-role exception (lasagna as only veg option). Score 5: correct math, explicit thresholds, class-appropriate actions, nuanced ravioli call. Score 3: right general classes but no thresholds/math shown, or ravioli handled as pure numbers (kill it) with no brand reasoning. Score 1: uses food-cost % as the ranking metric, or 'raise all prices'. RED FLAGS: calling ribeye a problem because 45% food cost; deleting the regulars' dish without addressing the fallout; no dollars anywhere.

prompt: Your 80-seat restaurant is fully booked this Saturday (two turns, ~180 covers). Three weeks ago, your sales manager also accepted a 120-guest offsite catering job for the same Saturday — drop-off buffet 25 minutes away, service window 18:00-18:30, $9,600 contract with a signed agreement, and the client just emailed: one guest has a severe tree-nut allergy, 'please ensure nothing served contains or touched nuts.' Your kitchen: one 10-burner range, two convection ovens, a 6-person BOH crew Saturday plus you. Walk through: whether you keep both commitments and how; the prep timeline (what cooks when); how you staff both without wrecking service; and the allergy protocol for the catering food specifically — prep, packing, transport, labeling, and what you tell the client in writing.
expected: Keep both — a signed $9,600 contract three weeks out and a booked dining room are both real commitments; the physics work IF the catering menu is engineered for it: buffet food that holds well and cooks ahead (braises, roasted vegetables, grain salads, sheet-pan proteins) — nothing à la minute. Timeline: Thursday — order/receive catering product separately labeled; Friday — bulk prep: braises fully cooked (they improve overnight), sauces, salads components, desserts; Saturday morning-to-14:00 — final catering production BEFORE dinner mise: proteins roasted, hot items into hotel pans, blast-chill or hot-hold decisions per item; 15:30-16:15 — pack with temp logs (hot >135°F in insulated carriers or cold <41°F with reheating instructions per the drop-off model); 16:45 — van leaves (25 min + setup buffer for the 18:00-18:30 window); the range/ovens are fully back to dinner service by 15:00, before the 17:00 first seating. Staffing: catering rides with 1 BOH (plus a driver/server for setup) OR you do the drop and are back by 18:00 — better: send your most senior prep cook, keep yourself on the line for 180 covers; dining room runs a slightly tightened menu Saturday (86 the two most labor-intense dishes) with 5 BOH. Allergy protocol — treat as allergen event, not a preference: menu audit for tree nuts INCLUDING hidden vectors (pesto, praline garnish, nut oils, dessert crusts, bought-in items' labels — check the bakery bread and the dessert supplier docs); ideal is a fully nut-free catering menu (removes cross-contact risk class entirely — one guest, 120 covers, just design nuts out); if any nut item stays, full segregation: prep first in the morning on sanitized surfaces before any nut product is opened, dedicated cutting boards/pans/utensils, gloves changed, items sealed and labeled before nut work starts. Label every pan ('contains'/'nut-free', prep date/time), pack the allergy-safe items physically separated (sealed, top of stack or separate carrier). In writing to the client: confirm the menu is prepared nut-free / identify any items that DO contain nuts, describe the segregation protocol, state honestly that a shared kitchen cannot guarantee zero cross-contact ('prepared in a facility that handles tree nuts') — never write the word 'guarantee' — and ask them to have the guest identify themselves to the on-site contact. Have the on-site server briefed on the allergy and on epi-awareness. What sinks a 3: no written client communication or promising a 'guarantee'; what sinks everything: nut items prepped alongside the catering food with a 'we'll be careful.'
rubric: Expert judge checklist: (1) keeps both commitments via menu engineering — catering food chosen to cook-ahead and hold, kitchen returned to dinner mise by a stated hour; (2) a real timeline with day-by-day prep and the equipment conflict resolved (catering production ends before dinner service ramps); (3) staffing split that doesn't gut the 180-cover line — senior prep cook or self on the drop, tightened dinner menu acknowledged; (4) allergen work is systematic: hidden-vector audit (oils, garnishes, bought-in items), prefers designing nuts out of the whole catering menu, otherwise first-in-the-morning segregation with dedicated equipment and sealed labeling; (5) transport food-safety mechanics present (hot-hold >135°F/cold <41°F, insulated carriers, temp log, labeling); (6) written client comms that disclose shared-facility reality and avoid 'guarantee', plus on-site briefing. Score 5: 1,2,4,6 solid with a plausible timeline. Score 3: keeps both and handles nuts respectfully but timeline is hand-wavy or transport temps missing. Score 1: cancels/subcontracts one event as the primary plan without engaging the logistics, or treats the allergy as 'no nuts in the recipes, done.' RED FLAGS: promising zero cross-contact in writing; catering hot food packed with no temp control mentioned; prepping nut desserts for the dining room alongside catering production without segregation.
